title: Loot boxes / gacha
evidenceNote: Replicated, meta-analytic correlation with problem gambling; effect stronger in adolescents.
summary: Paid, randomised reward containers whose contents — and often whose odds — are unknown before purchase.
howItWorks: Players spend real money (often via an intermediate premium currency) for a randomised draw. Variable-ratio reinforcement and near-miss framing encourage repeat purchases, and 'pity' counters extend the spend cycle.
whyHarmful: The structure is psychologically akin to gambling and shows a robust, replicated correlation with problem gambling — strongest among adolescents — with revenue concentrated in a small share of high-spenders. It is the clearest case of exploitation that persists even when fully transparent.
examples: Gacha 'banners' in mobile RPGs with rate-up characters
Card or skin packs bought with premium currency
Sports-title player packs in 'ultimate team' modes

title: Hidden / undisclosed odds
evidenceNote: Central to gambling-regulation analyses; disclosure is a leading policy remedy.
summary: The probability of outcomes in a paid randomised reward is withheld, buried, or hard to verify.
howItWorks: Drop rates are omitted, hidden in submenus, or stated in ways players cannot verify, so the true expected value of a purchase is unknowable at the point of sale.
whyHarmful: Without odds, players cannot form an accurate belief about what their money buys — a textbook deceptive failure of material transparency. Mandatory odds disclosure is among the most widely proposed regulatory fixes.
examples: A loot box that lists items but not their drop rates
Odds shown only in an external web page, not in the purchase flow

title: Premium-currency obfuscation
evidenceNote: Documented in player-perception and consumer-law analyses of mobile monetization.
summary: Real money is converted into in-game currency at non-round ratios that break the player's price intuition.
howItWorks: Purchases are denominated in gems/coins sold in bundles that never quite match item prices, leaving stranded balances and obscuring the real cost of any single item.
whyHarmful: The extra conversion step severs the felt link between money and goods, so players systematically under-perceive what they are spending — especially harmful for children and when combined with randomised rewards.
examples: Buying 1,000 gems for a 1,200-gem item, forcing another purchase
Multiple stacked currencies (coins, gems, tickets)

title: Bundle anchoring & decoys
evidenceNote: Consistent with player-reported pricing tactics; grounded in choice-architecture research.
summary: Tiered packs are priced to steer players toward a 'best value' middle option through anchoring and decoys.
howItWorks: An overpriced top tier and a token bottom tier make the target mid-tier look rational, while leftover balances nudge the next purchase.
whyHarmful: The arrangement exploits anchoring and the decoy effect to shift spending upward without adding real value, privileging the provider's interest over the player's.
examples: Starter / Value / Mega coin packs where 'Value' is the obvious pick
Limited 'bundle' deals that bundle in unwanted items

title: Pay-to-win
evidenceNote: Strong, consistent player-perceived unfairness in competitive contexts.
summary: Purchasable power converts money into competitive advantage, undermining the implicit contract of skill.
howItWorks: Players can buy items, stats, or progression that materially affect competitive outcomes against non-paying players.
whyHarmful: It breaks the fairness expectation at the heart of competitive play; players consistently rate it among the most unfair monetization practices, and it pressures spending to stay competitive.
examples: Buyable best-in-slot gear in competitive PvP
Paid stat boosts that decide ranked matches

title: Pay-to-skip / engineered grind
evidenceNote: Documented in exploitation-focused analyses of monetized game services.
summary: Progression is deliberately slowed so the game can sell time-savers that remove the friction it introduced.
howItWorks: Designers tune progression to be tedious, then offer paid shortcuts; the 'problem' and the 'solution' are both manufactured.
whyHarmful: It converts the player's time into a lever for extraction and shrinks the realistic free path, shading from manipulation into coercion when progression is effectively withheld until payment.
examples: Doubled XP or instant-build offers after a deliberately slow curve
Resource caps that make the free path impractical

title: FOMO / limited-time offers
evidenceNote: Recurring in player reports and early-childhood gaming studies.
summary: Artificial scarcity and urgency pressure players into purchases before a countdown expires.
howItWorks: Limited-time banners, expiring bundles, and countdown timers manufacture a fear of missing out that shortcuts deliberation.
whyHarmful: Time pressure at the decision point interferes with reasoning and pushes impulsive spending; data-triggered offers can target moments of heightened susceptibility.
examples: 'Only today!' starter bundles for new players
Rotating limited banners tied to events

title: Monetising basic quality-of-life
evidenceNote: Reported by players as an unfair, friction-for-sale tactic.
summary: The game charges to remove friction that the game itself introduced.
howItWorks: Basic conveniences — extra inventory, auto-collect, faster menus — are withheld and sold back as upgrades.
whyHarmful: It manufactures inconvenience to extract payment for relief, an asymmetric transfer of value that benefits the provider at the player's expense.
examples: Paid inventory expansions in games with deliberately tiny default storage
Selling an 'auto-play' for a grindy loop

title: Bait-and-switch / product not as expected
evidenceNote: Frequent in player reviews of mobile monetization.
summary: The advertised content or experience differs materially from what is actually delivered.
howItWorks: Ads or store pages depict gameplay, characters, or value that the installed product does not contain.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about what is being bought, the core of deception; players report frustration and a sense of being misled.
examples: Ads showing a puzzle minigame that barely appears in the real game
Store screenshots not representative of actual play

title: Predatory / forced advertising
evidenceNote: Prevalent in children's apps per reliable content-coding studies.
summary: Unskippable or rewarded ads — sometimes disguised as content — are bundled into progression.
howItWorks: Players must watch ads to continue or to claim rewards, and ads are often styled to look like gameplay, especially in children's titles.
whyHarmful: It forces attention and, when disguised, deceives — disproportionately reaching children who cannot reliably distinguish ads from play.
examples: Mandatory ad before each level retry
An 'ad' that mimics the game's own UI

title: Accidental-purchase / default-to-purchase UI
evidenceNote: Documented in 3D/UGC player-perception work and consumer-protection enforcement.
summary: Purchase is the default or easily mis-tapped path, so spending happens without express, informed consent.
howItWorks: Buy buttons sit where 'continue' usually is, confirmation is minimal, and stored payment makes a tap a charge.
whyHarmful: It manufactures purchases the player did not intend — a consent failure that has drawn major regulatory action, and is acute for children.
examples: A 'claim reward' button that actually buys currency
One-tap purchases with no meaningful confirmation

title: Subscription / battle-pass traps
evidenceNote: Analysed in free-to-play transition research.
summary: Easy entry and obstructed cancellation, with 'earned' value that expires if you stop paying or playing.
howItWorks: Battle passes and subscriptions create loss-averse commitment: progress and rewards are time-boxed, and cancelling is harder than subscribing.
whyHarmful: It withholds and penalises exit, the coercive core, converting fear of wasted investment into recurring spend and obligatory play.
examples: A season pass whose unspent tiers vanish at reset
Subscriptions easy to start but buried to cancel

title: Grinding / engineered repetition
evidenceNote: Often normalised as 'progression'; harmful chiefly when monetized.
summary: Repetitive effort is inflated to extend play or to drive pay-to-skip purchases.
howItWorks: Reward curves are tuned so meaningful progress requires large amounts of repetitive activity.
whyHarmful: Benign as pacing, it becomes exploitative when paired with paid shortcuts or when the dose is tuned for retention over enjoyment. A clear case where context, not the mechanic, decides harm.
examples: Hundreds of identical runs for one upgrade
XP curves that flatten right before a paywall

title: Energy / wait timers (appointment mechanics)
evidenceNote: Classic temporal dark pattern; documented in early-childhood gaming.
summary: Play is gated by real-time cooldowns that can be bypassed for money.
howItWorks: An energy/lives meter depletes and refills on a real-world clock, prompting players to return on the game's schedule or pay to continue.
whyHarmful: It colonises the player's real time and converts impatience into spend; appointment scheduling is especially hard on children who lack control over when they play.
examples: Five 'lives' that refill one per 30 minutes
Timed building/crafting that costs gems to finish now

title: Daily login / streaks
evidenceNote: Loss-aversion mechanic noted across temporal-pattern and child-app studies.
summary: Escalating rewards and loss-aversion penalties punish missing a day.
howItWorks: Consecutive-day rewards grow, and breaking the streak resets them — turning a habit into an obligation.
whyHarmful: It manufactures anxiety about missing out and binds daily attention through loss aversion rather than genuine enjoyment.
examples: A 30-day reward calendar that resets on a single miss
Streak counters with escalating bonuses

title: Time fog / playtime opacity
evidenceNote: Part of the newer attention-capture deceptive-design literature.
summary: Elapsed time and session length are concealed so players lose track of how long they have played.
howItWorks: The interface omits clocks, session timers, or stopping cues, keeping the player in a timeless flow.
whyHarmful: Hiding time obscures a material fact about the cost of play, degrading the player's ability to self-regulate attention.
examples: No in-game clock or session timer
Seamless level chaining with no natural stop

title: Interruptible-but-sticky loops
evidenceNote: Attention-capture mechanics; evidence base still developing.
summary: Auto-advancing or endless content resists natural stopping points.
howItWorks: The next round, reward, or item loads automatically, removing the friction that would prompt a player to stop.
whyHarmful: By engineering away stopping cues it captures attention beyond the player's intention, a temporal harm to digital wellbeing.
examples: Auto-queue into the next match
Continuous reward feeds with no end screen

title: Social obligation / guilt
evidenceNote: Reported across UGC and competitive-fairness studies.
summary: The design leverages teammates' dependence to compel continued play or spending.
howItWorks: Shared goals, guild quotas, or co-op stakes make stepping away feel like letting others down.
whyHarmful: It applies interpersonal pressure that overrides the player's own preference about when to stop, shading into coercion through social cost.
examples: Guild contribution quotas with penalties
Co-op events where dropping out hurts friends

title: Parasocial-character pressure
evidenceNote: Documented as a prevalent manipulative tactic in children's apps.
summary: Beloved characters urge purchases or continued play, exploiting parasocial attachment.
howItWorks: A trusted mascot pleads, looks sad, or rewards the player for spending, leveraging the emotional bond — especially powerful with children.
whyHarmful: It exploits a developmental vulnerability: children attribute feelings and authority to characters and cannot easily resist their 'requests'.
examples: A sad mascot asking the child not to leave
A character who 'gives a gift' that opens the store

title: Gifting / invitation spam (social pyramid)
evidenceNote: Long-noted virality tactic; systematic game-specific evidence still thin.
summary: Progress is tied to recruiting or pestering friends.
howItWorks: Rewards or gating require inviting contacts, sharing, or gifting, turning the player into a distribution channel.
whyHarmful: It instrumentalises relationships and can harvest contact data, transferring social capital and privacy to the provider.
examples: 'Invite 3 friends to unlock'
Daily gift-spam to a contact list

title: Impersonation / disguised system-as-friend
evidenceNote: Reported in 3D/UGC player-perception research as interpersonal persuasion.
summary: System messages are styled to look like communication from a peer.
howItWorks: Automated prompts are framed as if a friend or another player sent them, borrowing social trust.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about who is speaking, using manufactured social proof to steer choices the player would otherwise resist.
examples: A 'message' from a fake teammate urging a purchase
NPC framed as a real co-player

title: Monetised social status
evidenceNote: Linked to gamblification and competitive-fairness perceptions.
summary: Visible status is sold to exploit peer comparison and the desire to belong.
howItWorks: Cosmetics, badges, or ranks that signal spending are made visible to others, turning social comparison into a sales driver.
whyHarmful: It monetises belonging and esteem, pressuring spend through peer comparison rather than intrinsic value — acute among younger players.
examples: Conspicuous paid skins that mark non-payers
Leaderboards that reward spending visibly

title: Variable-ratio reward / near-miss
evidenceNote: Core gambling psychology; well-evidenced reinforcement mechanism.
summary: Slot-machine-like reinforcement and 'almost won' framing drive repeated attempts.
howItWorks: Rewards arrive on an unpredictable schedule, and near-miss outcomes are highlighted to imply success was close.
whyHarmful: Variable-ratio schedules are the most persistent form of reinforcement, and near-miss framing fuels chasing — the engine behind randomised-reward harm.
examples: A pull that lands just short of the rare item
Reels or spins that frequently show near-wins

title: Sunk-cost / entrapment
evidenceNote: Mechanism described across predatory-monetization analyses.
summary: Designs make prior investment justify further spending or time.
howItWorks: Progress, collections, or partial purchases are framed so quitting feels like wasting what is already invested.
whyHarmful: It exploits the sunk-cost fallacy to keep players committing more money and time against their own interest.
examples: 'You're 80% to the prize — keep going'
Collections that feel wasteful to abandon

title: Illusion of control / skill framing
evidenceNote: Identified in exploitation analyses of chance-based mechanics.
summary: Chance outcomes are presented as if skill or choice could influence them.
howItWorks: Stop buttons, 'pick a box', or timing flourishes imply agency over results that are actually random.
whyHarmful: It fosters a false belief that effort or skill changes the odds, encouraging continued play and spend on what is pure chance.
examples: A 'tap to stop' wheel whose result is predetermined
Choose-your-box reveals over fixed odds

title: Aesthetic / sensory manipulation
evidenceNote: Reinforcing 'celebration' effects noted in gambling-adjacent design.
summary: Celebratory audio-visual feedback reinforces spending and reward moments.
howItWorks: Lights, fanfares, and animations amplify every reward — sometimes even net-loss outcomes — to feel like a win.
whyHarmful: Sensory reinforcement strengthens the spend-reward loop and can disguise losses as wins, nudging continued play.
examples: Big celebratory effects for a low-value pull
Fanfare on a 'win' that cost more than it returned

title: Aggressive nagging / interruptive upsell
evidenceNote: Nagging is a well-established cross-domain dark-pattern family.
summary: Frequent pop-ups repeatedly steer the player to the store.
howItWorks: Interruptive prompts surface offers on launch, between levels, and at idle moments, wearing down resistance.
whyHarmful: Repetition imposes an attention tax and pressures purchases through persistence rather than value.
examples: A store pop-up every time you open the app
Offers interrupting the flow between levels

title: Hidden costs
evidenceNote: Canonical dark pattern carried into games.
summary: The true cost of a purchase is revealed late, or never.
howItWorks: Additional charges, currency conversions, or requirements appear only after the player is committed.
whyHarmful: Withholding a material fact about price deceives the player and undermines informed consent.
examples: A 'free' item that needs paid currency to use
Costs that surface only at the final step

title: Confirmshaming
evidenceNote: Well-documented interface-interference pattern.
summary: Opt-out wording is laden with guilt to discourage the protective choice.
howItWorks: The decline button is phrased to make saying no feel foolish or shameful ('No thanks, I hate saving money').
whyHarmful: It manipulates through emotion rather than reasons, eroding autonomy at the point of choice.
examples: 'No, I don't want to get stronger' to decline an offer
Guilt-worded unsubscribe prompts

title: Misdirection / false visual hierarchy
evidenceNote: Recognised across mobile-app dark-pattern studies.
summary: Visual salience steers the player toward the provider-preferred option.
howItWorks: Color, size, and placement make the spend or opt-in button dominant while the alternative is muted.
whyHarmful: It biases choices by exploiting attention rather than informing it, quietly redirecting decisions.
examples: A bright 'Buy' button beside a greyed-out 'No'
Pre-highlighted most-expensive option

title: Obstructed exit / cancellation (sludge)
evidenceNote: Sludge / cancellation friction documented in gambling and account-closure studies.
summary: Asymmetric friction makes quitting, refunding, or disabling far harder than starting.
howItWorks: Cancellation is buried, multi-step, or routed through obstacles, while signup is one tap.
whyHarmful: It penalises and withholds exit — the coercive core — trapping spend and time and overriding the player's choice to leave.
examples: Refund links hidden deep in settings
Cancellation that demands multiple confirmations and waits

title: Disguised ads / content
evidenceNote: Prevalent in children's apps per reliable content analysis.
summary: Ads are styled as gameplay or rewards so the player cannot tell promotion from play.
howItWorks: Advertising is camouflaged within the game's own visual language, blurring the line between content and commerce.
whyHarmful: It deceives about what is an advertisement — especially harmful for children, who struggle to recognise persuasive intent.
examples: A 'reward' button that opens an ad/store
Ad banners drawn to look like collectible items

title: Value obfuscation / false narratives
evidenceNote: Reported in consumer-law and player-perception work on monetization.
summary: What a purchase actually yields is misrepresented through framing or false comparison.
howItWorks: Inflated 'original' prices, fake discounts, or vague yields make purchases look more valuable than they are.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about value, distorting the player's cost-benefit judgement at the point of sale.
examples: '80% off' against a price never charged
Bundles whose stated value double-counts items

title: Power creep
evidenceNote: Documented in exploitation and player-perception analyses of monetised games.
summary: Continually releasing more powerful paid items so previously bought ones become obsolete, pressuring repeat purchases to keep up.
howItWorks: Each new release — characters, gear, cards — is stronger than the last, so the value of earlier purchases steadily decays and players must keep buying the newest tier to stay effective.
whyHarmful: It manufactures planned obsolescence of the player's own investments, converting fear of falling behind into recurring spend; in competitive modes it shades into pay-to-win and erodes the fairness contract.
examples: Gacha RPGs where new banner units outclass older ones
Card games that print a stronger set each season
Sports titles whose new player items outshine last year's

title: Pay-for-early-access
evidenceNote: Player-perceived unfairness of timed advantages; named in the Game-check report.
summary: Selling early access to content, weapons, or updates so patience becomes a purchasable advantage.
howItWorks: New items or updates release first to payers (or pass-holders) and only later to everyone else; in competitive games this confers a temporary edge, and everywhere it leverages FOMO and status.
whyHarmful: When early items affect gameplay it blurs into pay-to-win and undermines competitive fairness, and the “it'll be free later” framing normalises impulse spending driven by urgency rather than value.
examples: Early weapon unlocks in live-service shooters
Battle-pass “early access” tiers
Pre-release character bundles

title: Creator tipping & crowdfunded content
evidenceNote: Newer monetisation surface; UGC-monetisation research and the Game-check report.
summary: Routing real money to creators or crowdfunding unreleased content, where prosocial “support” framing lowers price scrutiny.
howItWorks: Platforms enable tips, creator subscriptions, or backer crowdfunding for features and exclusive items, often for goods that are unreleased or perpetually in development; “support the creator” framing and exclusive-backer rewards add FOMO.
whyHarmful: The generosity framing can mask open-ended spending and whaling, minors may not grasp that tips are real money, and crowdfunding exclusive items exploits scarcity while shifting financial risk onto the fan.
examples: In-platform creator tipping and commissions on UGC platforms
Crowdfunded ships or items for unreleased games
Paid creator-subscription perks

title: Can't pause or save
evidenceNote: Inability to pause is a child-app harm typology (Radesky); children's-rights and temporal-design work.
summary: Designs that prevent safely stopping — no pause or save, or progress lost (or attacked) when you leave — so players can't quit on their own terms.
howItWorks: Sessions can't be paused or saved midway, multiplayer or timed modes penalise leaving, and some persistent games let others raid your base while you're offline and then sell “protection.” Stopping carries a real penalty.
whyHarmful: It strips control over when to stop — acute for children who don't choose when they must log off, causing distress and family conflict — and turns the inability to safely pause into compulsive, anxiety-driven play.
examples: Roblox experiences with no mid-session save
Match or raid modes that punish quitting
Base-builders where you're raided offline unless you buy a shield

title: Content treadmill
evidenceNote: Temporal-design literature; the Game-check report names continuous content refresh as a retention engine.
summary: A relentless cadence of new seasons, stories, and limited updates keeps players returning so they never fall behind an ever-moving target.
howItWorks: Live-service games add content on a fixed cadence so the game is never “finished”; combined with seasonal and limited-time framing, disengaging feels like permanent loss.
whyHarmful: The never-ending cadence removes natural stopping points, inflates long-run time spent, and feeds perpetual catch-up pressure that also sustains monetisation.
examples: Seasonal storylines in live-service shooters
Roughly ten-week content refreshes
Expansion cadences that reset the meta

title: Reciprocity
evidenceNote: Reciprocity-norm exploitation; UGC-monetisation research and the Game-check report.
summary: Giving the player or their friends a free gift to create a felt obligation to give back — by spending, playing, or recruiting.
howItWorks: The game or other players send unsolicited gifts or help, triggering the reciprocity norm so the recipient feels obliged to reciprocate with time, money, or invitations.
whyHarmful: It weaponises a deep social norm to extract behaviour the player wouldn't otherwise choose, and entangles real relationships in the monetisation loop.
examples: Free “gifts” that prompt a return purchase
Guild help that obliges reciprocation
Daily gifting chains between friends

title: Encourages anti-social behaviour
evidenceNote: Community-catalogued (DarkPattern.games); social-harm evidence in games is still developing.
summary: Designs that reward or normalise toxic, aggressive, or anti-social behaviour in order to drive engagement.
howItWorks: Competitive incentives, unmoderated interaction, and reward structures that favour dominance can make harassment, griefing, or exclusion a route to status or progress.
whyHarmful: It externalises harm onto other players — especially vulnerable and younger ones — degrading wellbeing and community health, and can become a vector for broader social harms.
examples: Reward systems that incentivise griefing
Unmoderated voice and chat tied to competitive pressure
Status earned by humiliating others

title: Manufactured competition
evidenceNote: Competitive-fairness perceptions and gamblification; the Game-check report flags ranked/leaderboard pressure.
summary: Leaderboards, resetting ranks, and rivalry framing turn social comparison into a driver of compulsive play and spending.
howItWorks: Ranked ladders, periodically reset leaderboards, and visible rivalries pressure players to keep grinding — or paying — to climb or to avoid dropping.
whyHarmful: It exploits status anxiety and peer comparison to sustain engagement beyond intent, and ties into pay-to-win and monetised status when rank can be bought.
examples: Seasonal ranked resets with exclusive rewards
Friend leaderboards on idle and mobile games
Buyable rank boosts

title: Licensed-IP collaboration FOMO
evidenceNote: Player-perceived FOMO monetisation; the Game-check report names brand collaborations.
summary: Time-limited cosmetics tied to a popular external brand or franchise drive purchases through fandom and scarcity rather than gameplay value.
howItWorks: The game partners with a real-world IP — films, anime, musicians — for limited-time themed items or events; external fandom plus “only now” scarcity creates urgency independent of in-game utility.
whyHarmful: It targets emotional attachment to a franchise to drive impulse spending, punishes hesitation, and is especially potent for young fans; repeated crossovers normalise frequent cosmetic spend.
examples: Franchise crossover skins from films or comics
Anime collaboration banners
Music-artist in-game events

title: Involuntary social ranking / identity labels
evidenceNote: Directly observable in social-platform features; broader support from social-comparison, social-media dark-pattern, and consent literature. Game-specific evidence is still developing.
summary: The system assigns relationship labels, closeness ranks, or social-cluster positions to people from behavioural data they did not choose to make socially meaningful.
howItWorks: Interaction frequency, message history, gifting, guild activity, or proximity data are converted into labels and ranks such as best friend, mutual best friend, top supporter, inner circle, or a position in another person's social orbit. The label can feel authoritative even when the metric is partial, private, volatile, or poorly explained.
whyHarmful: It turns inferred behaviour into identity and relationship status without meaningful consent from everyone affected. The result can expose relational metadata, intensify comparison and insecurity, provoke conflict between friends or partners, and pressure people to change their behaviour to regain a preferred rank or label.
examples: Snapchat Friend Solar Systems ranking a user as a planet in someone else's Best Friends list
Automatic friend emojis that label best friends, mutual besties, or streak status
Game friend leaderboards that rank closeness by gifts, messages, or co-play
Guild or creator-community tiers that publicly mark insiders and outsiders

title: Endowed progress
evidenceNote: Endowed-progress / Zeigarnik effects; temporal-design literature and the Game-check report.
summary: Giving players an artificial head-start or visible partial progress so the urge to complete it pulls them onward.
howItWorks: Progress bars start pre-filled, badges show “almost there,” and tracked partial completion exploits the endowed-progress and Zeigarnik effects to compel finishing.
whyHarmful: It manufactures a completion drive from an artificial gap, channelling time and money toward goals the player didn't independently set; paired with time limits it becomes urgent.
examples: Pre-stamped “loyalty” progress
Battle-pass tiers shown most of the way done
Badge grids with conspicuous gaps

title: Collection & completionism pressure
evidenceNote: Sunk-cost and collection-completion drives; player-perception and Game-check report.
summary: A visible, incomplete collection — roster, index, grid — compels players to keep playing or paying to complete the set.
howItWorks: The game surfaces sets with conspicuous empty slots, exploiting completion psychology; missing entries are often gated behind grind, limited-time windows, or gacha.
whyHarmful: The pull to finish a set drives disproportionate grind and spend, entrenches sunk-cost (“I'm 90% there”), and is potent for children — spend driven by an artificial gap, not value.
examples: Creature or character indexes with rare gaps
Unlock grids completed via gacha
Limited-time “complete the collection” events

title: Optimism & frequency bias
evidenceNote: Cognitive-bias exploitation around chance outcomes; loot-box and exploitation literature.
summary: Framing that inflates perceived chances of winning — emphasising wins and near-misses, downplaying losses — to exploit optimism and frequency illusions.
howItWorks: Celebratory feedback for wins, salient near-misses, and broadcast of others' rare successes make good outcomes feel more frequent and likely than they actually are.
whyHarmful: It distorts the player's estimate of the odds, encouraging continued play and spend on what is mostly chance — a belief-level (deceptive) effect layered on reinforcement (manipulative).
examples: “So close!” near-miss reels
Server-wide announcements of rare wins
Highlighting wins while hiding the misses

title: Fake social proof
evidenceNote: Cross-domain dark pattern (Mathur et al.); catalogued by deceptive.design.
summary: Fabricated or unverifiable signals of others' activity — “1M players bought this!”, fake live counters — used to pressure decisions.
howItWorks: The interface shows invented or unverifiable activity, purchase counts, or “players online” figures to imply popularity and manufacture trust and urgency.
whyHarmful: It induces a false belief about how others behave, exploiting conformity to steer spending and engagement — deception about the social context.
examples: Fake “X people are viewing or buying this” counters
Inflated player-count or popularity badges
Bogus testimonial pop-ups

title: Comparison prevention
evidenceNote: Cross-domain dark pattern (Mathur et al.; Gray et al.); catalogued by deceptive.design.
summary: Making it hard to compare prices, odds, or options so players can't judge value.
howItWorks: Currencies, bundle contents, and shifting offers are structured so equivalent options can't be lined up side by side; like-for-like comparison is obstructed.
whyHarmful: Blocking comparison defeats informed choice and hides poor value — a key precondition for overspending, closely tied to currency obfuscation and decoy pricing.
examples: Bundles priced in mismatched currencies
No way to compare odds or value across packs
Offers that change before you can compare them

title: Offer-wall / cross-promotion redirection
evidenceNote: Children's-app advertising harms (Radesky); named explicitly in the Game-check report.
summary: Dangling an in-game reward for going to another app or game and spending money or time there, confirmed by a third-party tracker.
howItWorks: An “offer wall” sends the player to a secondary product to install, play, or purchase; an intermediary SDK verifies completion and grants premium currency in the original game.
whyHarmful: It funnels players into third-party spending and time with opaque data-sharing they rarely understand, and is especially exploitative of children chasing the reward.
examples: “Earn free gems” offer walls
Install-and-spend cross-promotions
Rewards for watching external streams

title: Personalised spend-optimisation
evidenceNote: Precautionary severe rating based on the potential harm of covert individual targeting; game-specific evidence remains emerging.
summary: Silently using a player's behavioural data to tune offers, prices, odds, difficulty, or matchmaking to maximise that individual's spending.
howItWorks: Dynamic systems informed by play and purchase history adjust what each player sees — offers, “frustration → sell-a-fix” difficulty, or matchmaking that surrounds them with spenders — optimised per person to increase spend, invisibly.
whyHarmful: Invisible, individualised targeting defeats informed consent and comparison, can exploit identified vulnerabilities (a data-protection concern), and manufactures false social proof; children and at-risk players cannot detect or resist it. The severity rating is precautionary: the harm could be severe when such targeting is deployed at scale, even though the public game-specific evidence base is still emerging.
examples: Behaviour-tuned offers and dynamic pricing
Difficulty tuned to sell a fix
Matchmaking that normalises spending

title: Trick wording / misleading copy
evidenceNote: Well-established cross-domain deceptive-design pattern; game-specific evidence overlaps with purchase, consent, and interface-interference studies.
summary: Confusing, ambiguous, or expectation-violating wording makes the player take an action they did not mean to take.
howItWorks: Button labels, toggles, prompts, or shop copy are phrased so a quick scan suggests one meaning while the actual consequence is different. In games this can attach purchases, data sharing, notifications, account linking, or unwanted difficulty and matchmaking choices to wording that looks harmless or protective.
whyHarmful: The harm is epistemic and autonomy-based: the player is not choosing the action they believe they are choosing. When attached to premium currency, subscriptions, ads, data permissions, or account-linking flows, misleading copy can turn ordinary scan-reading into financial or privacy harm.
examples: A claim button that quietly spends premium currency rather than collecting a free reward
Double-negative consent toggles for tracking, notifications, or personalised offers
A decline option worded so it appears to reject a penalty but actually accepts one
Quest or event copy that hides a required ad watch, account link, or purchase behind vague wording

title: Bad defaults / preselection
evidenceNote: Gray et al. identify bad defaults and preselection as interface-interference patterns; game-specific evidence overlaps with accidental purchase and consent-flow studies.
summary: The provider-preferred option is already selected or treated as the normal path, so inaction becomes consent, spending, or data sharing.
howItWorks: Settings, purchase confirmations, account-linking prompts, or privacy choices ship with the risky option preselected. The player must notice and actively undo the default, often while trying to continue play.
whyHarmful: Defaults exploit inertia and misplaced trust: players often assume defaults are safe or recommended. In games, this can turn a quick continue action into opt-in tracking, notifications, matchmaking exposure, subscription renewal, or a paid purchase path.
examples: A paid bundle tier preselected in a shop modal
Personalised offers or tracking enabled by default
Auto-renewal or notifications preselected during account linking
A default matchmaking or social-visibility setting that exposes more than expected

title: Choice overload / option flooding
evidenceNote: Named in Gray et al.'s ontology; game-specific evidence is emerging through shop, bundle, and settings-interface analyses.
summary: The interface floods the player with too many overlapping options, currencies, bundles, settings, or offers to compare meaningfully.
howItWorks: A shop, event screen, or settings flow presents dense, shifting, and partially incomparable options. The resulting cognitive load makes the default, highlighted, or most urgent option more likely to be accepted.
whyHarmful: Choice overload undermines informed consent without hiding every detail. Players technically see options, but the volume and structure of information make meaningful comparison unrealistic, especially under time pressure or during play interruption.
examples: A live-service shop with dozens of time-limited bundles and currencies
Settings split across many screens with repeated prompts to accept the default
Event reward screens with many exchange rates and upgrade paths
A consent or account-linking flow with dense toggles and unclear tradeoffs

title: Forced registration / data disclosure
evidenceNote: Gray et al. group forced registration, privacy zuckering, friend spam, and address-book leeching under forced communication or disclosure.
summary: Access to play, rewards, or social features is made conditional on creating an account, linking an identity, or sharing unnecessary personal or contact data.
howItWorks: The game blocks or degrades functionality until the player registers, links a platform account, grants contact permissions, uploads age or identity data, or shares friend-network data. The request is framed as necessary even when the data primarily benefits growth, advertising, analytics, or cross-platform tracking.
whyHarmful: The design converts access pressure into consent. It can expose personal and social data, affect non-consenting contacts, and make refusal feel like losing access to play or progress rather than exercising a real privacy choice.
examples: A game reward gated behind linking a social account
Contact-list access framed as necessary to find friends
UGC access that requires unnecessary profile or identity data
Friend-invite flows that collect contacts before clearly explaining how they will be used

title: Language inaccessibility / complex copy
evidenceNote: Gray et al.'s ontology names wrong-language and complex-language patterns; game-specific evidence overlaps with trick wording and consent-flow research.
summary: Important purchase, privacy, odds, or consent information is presented in language the player cannot reasonably understand.
howItWorks: Disclosures use legalistic, technical, untranslated, or age-inappropriate wording while the action button remains simple and salient. The result is a consent or purchase flow that appears transparent but is not intelligible to the affected player.
whyHarmful: Disclosure does not protect autonomy if the player cannot understand it. In games, complex or inaccessible wording is especially harmful for children, cross-language audiences, and fast-paced play contexts where players are pushed to continue quickly.
examples: Loot-box odds or subscription terms written in dense legal language
Tracking or personalised-offer consent shown only in the wrong language
Child-facing purchase prompts with adult legal vocabulary
Account-linking terms hidden behind untranslated platform jargon

title: Feedforward ambiguity / unclear consequences
evidenceNote: Gray et al. introduce feedforward ambiguity as a pattern; game-specific support comes from player-perception work on deceptive gameplay and interface cues.
summary: The interface fails to make clear what a button, prompt, or action will actually do before the player commits.
howItWorks: Labels, icons, animations, or placement suggest one outcome while the actual outcome is different or materially more consequential. Unlike broad trick wording, the problem is the missing or misleading feedforward cue about what will happen next.
whyHarmful: Games often ask for rapid, repeated action. When consequence cues are ambiguous, players can spend currency, consume scarce resources, reveal data, start timers, or change irreversible settings without a fair chance to predict the result.
examples: A glowing 'open' affordance that consumes premium currency without saying so
A reroll button that spends a rare item without previewing the cost
A social-share icon that posts publicly rather than opening a private invite
A claim prompt that starts a timed event instead of merely collecting a reward
